coredata.dc:title: Diamond quantum dots: Room-temperature synthesis, concurrent multiband luminescence, and origins of surface defects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenxia
Wang, Zhen
Wang, Yuchan
Yuan, Suzhen
Zhang, Li
Qi, Fei
coredata.dc:description: Diamond quantum dots (diamond QDs) have attracted great interest on account of their excellent physical and chemical properties. Here, we synthesized the diamond QDs 2–8 nm in diameter through alkaline assisted oxidation at room temperature. This composite method fairly original, simple, low-cost, and eco-friendly. High-resolution TEM, XRD and XPS confirm the diamond structure of the synthesized nanocrystals. The diamond QDs exhibit concurrent multiband luminescence across the whole visible region. The study based on the photoluminescence, UV–Vis absorption, and Fourier-transform infrared reveals that the fluorescence emission originates from the C–O–C and C(O)O related surface defects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diamond quantum dots
Room-temperature synthesis
Concurrent multiband luminescence
Surface defects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for synthesis of diamond quantum dots.

(a) The TEM image of diamond QDs. (b) The high-resolution image of some particles. Several lattice spacings are shown. (c) Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the diamond QDs. (d) Size distribution of the diamond QDs.
(a) XRD pattern of diamond QDs. (b) FTIR spectra of diamond QDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s of the diamond QDs.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) and (b) an enlarged PL spectrum with excitation wavelength between 280 and 340 of the diamond QDs. (c) PL excitation (PLE) spectra and (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the QDs.
(a) Stokes shift versus excitation wavelength and (b) PL intensity versus excitation wavelength at different emission wavelength for the diamond QDs.
Indexing of electron diffraction patterns of diamond QDs shown in Fig. 2.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: dexp refer to measured values for diamond QDs and dlit refer to literature values for diamond structure from the literature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Indexing of FTIR spectra of diamond QDs shown in Fig. 3b.

head.ce:title: Diamond quantum dots: Room-temperature synthesis, concurrent multiband luminescence, and origins of surface defects

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diamond quantum dots (diamond QDs) have attracted great interest on account of their excellent physical and chemical properties. Here, we synthesized the diamond QDs 2–8 nm in diameter through alkaline assisted oxidation at room temperature. This composite method fairly original, simple, low-cost, and eco-friendly. High-resolution TEM, XRD and XPS confirm the diamond structure of the synthesized nanocrystals. The diamond QDs exhibit concurrent multiband luminescence across the whole visible region. The study based on the photoluminescence, UV–Vis absorption, and Fourier-transform infrared reveals that the fluorescence emission originates from the C–O–C and C(O)O related surface defects.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), including the common graphite quantum dots, diamond quantum dots, and the scarce C8-structured carbon quantum dots have attracted tremendous interest because of their outstanding properties, such as high stability [1,2], environment-friendliness [3], excellent optical properties [4,5], optoelectrical conversion ability [6], and good biocompatibility [7–9]. CQDs have great impacts on different applications in bioimaging [8,9], fluorescent probes for various ions [9–13], sensors [14–16], and delivery of drugs [17]. They are can serve as the catalyst for H2 evolution and organics degradation [18–22]. CQDs find application in light emitting diodes and solar cells [23–26]. In view of this, a variety of preparation methods have been developed, which include arc discharge, chemical oxidation, laser ablation, electrochemical corrosion, thermal combustion, hydrothermal and solvothermal approaches. As a metastable state of carbon allotrope, the diamond QDs are usually synthesized by the detonation of carbon-containing explosives [27], chemical vapour deposition (CVD) [28], high-energy ball milling of high-pressure high-temperature diamond microcrystals [29], irradiation of graphite, and laser ablation [30], etc. These preparation methods are high demanding on the equipment, and go through a complicated process. Therefore, the development of a simple and cost-effective method is urgently required.

The optical property of CQDs has been a research focus. The proposed mechanisms vary from quantum confinement effect, local fluorophores, element doping to surface defects [31]. To be specific, the carbon-core states fluorescence center is from the band gap of conjugated π-domains [32–34], and the band gap fluorescence origins from the sp2 carbon clusters dispersed in the sp3 carbon skeleton [35], which are the two typical manifestations of quantum effects. Some small molecules or fluorophores similar to dye molecules connected on the surface or interior of the carbon backbone can exhibit fluorescence emission [36,37]. Another fluorescence mechanism is from the effects of doping with other elements to alter structural defects [38]. Surface states mechanism was considered to be the key fluorescence mechanism of CQDs. Surface defects are mainly generated from surface oxidation, which produces a series of emission traps [39]. Due to the diversity and complexity of surface defect states, luminescence of defect state is usually characterized by excitation-dependent behavior and multicolor emission [40]. So far, although there has been a lot of research on CQDs, results about the fluorescence origin of CQDs are still the subject of debate and need further research. Herein, we report a quite simple method to synthesize the diamond QDs with multicolor emission. Our study reveals that the luminescence in the diamond QDs originates from the C–O–C and C(O)O related surface defects.

As the dehydrator and oxidizer of some carbohydrates (such as cellulose acetate [41]) sodium hydroxide is a magic catalyst, However, what is different now, in this study, the carbon quantum dots were synthesized at room temperature and with the most common carbohydrate. The preparation method does not require heating at high temperature and high pressure, or there is no subsequent surface passivation treatment. As is shown in Fig. 1 , the diamond QDs were synthesized by alkali-assisted carbonization of glucose under room temperature. Specifically, 1 g sodium hydroxide was dissolved in 20 ml deionized water, then 3 g glucose was added into the above solution through less mass and high frequency, meanwhile, stirring with a glass rod to accelerate the dissolution. Afterward, the glass beaker containing the solution was sealed with plastic wrap and stood at room temperature (around 22 °C) for a few hours. The resultant solution underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 min, and then the supernatant that contains the diamond QDs was separated out. The resulting colloidal solution of quantum dots without any treatment is highly alkaline and wait for the structural and optical characterization analysis.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM–200CX TEM operating at 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted on a Flex-Axiom AFM equipped with a C3000 controllers. X–ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using a Smartlab (3) diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrophotometer. The X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Ka source. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a Fluorolog 3–TCSPC spectrofluorometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON) equipped with a xenon lamp and a NanoLED–375 L H (pulse duration: 750 ps). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U–3900 UV/Vis spectrophotometer.

Fig. S1 b, Fig. S1c, and Fig. 2 a display the TEM images with different scales of the synthesized diamond QDs. The nanoparticles are nearly spherical. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show that the diamond CDs have clear crystalline lattice fringes (Fig. S1a and Fig. 2b.), indicating that they are single crystals. It should be specially note that the nanoparticles can withstand the high-energy electron beams for about 1 min without phase transition or decomposition. This indirectly indicates that the nanoparticles have a certain stability. The lattice spacing is measured to be around 0.203/0.205 Å, being consistent with that of the (111) planes of nanodiamonds [42]. The selected area electron diffraction pattern further confirms the diamond structure of the nanoparticles (Fig. 2c). The calculated lattice spacings acquired from the diffraction pattern are all listed in Table 1 . These values are consistent with those of the diamond structure within the allowable error range. As shown in Fig. 2d, the diamond QDs have diameters in the range of 2.0–8.0 nm and the most probable diameter is about 4.5 nm. AFM images (Figs. S2a and b) show that their topographic heights were between 2.2 and 6.0 nm, consistent with the results of the TEM.

The X-ray diffraction measurement further confirms the diamond structure of the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD spectra of the diamond QDs powders. Three diffraction peaks are roughly identified, which agree with those of the diamond film in peak position. In the patterns, the peaks are wide and overlap with each other owing to the small particle size and existence of some amorphous nanoparticles. These are consistent with the result of the SAED pattern with blurry clouds surrounding the sharp rings. Fig. 3b displays the Fourier transform infrared absorption (FTIR) spectra of the diamond QDs. All the peaks are marked by English letters, and the correspondence between the peaks and the signals is listed in Table 2 . One of the most obvious features of the spectrum is that there are three strong absorption bands: the peaks at around 1035/1080/1120 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–O–C stretching vibrations [43,50], the signal at 1410 cm−1 arises from the CO stretching vibration or the O–H in-plane deformation [4], and the band at 1598–1632 cm−1 is attributed to the contribution of the CO (in COOˉ), CC and O–H absorption signal [46]. Besides, several other bands were observed in the diamond QDs, such as = C–O–C (1232 cm−1), C–H (1333 and 2935 cm−1) [45], CH3/C–O (1367 cm−1) [44], and O–H (3442 cm−1) [47–49]. These infrared absorption features confirm the existence of the CO, C–O–C and the CC bonds on the CQDs surfaces and these functional groups are stable under alkaline conditions.

Fig. S3 shows the Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDS) spectrum of the diamond QDs. In addition to the signal of copper from the substrate material, signals from C, O and N appear in the spectrum. Compared with the strong signal peak of carbon and oxygen, the signal of nitrogen is quite weak. The atomic percentage of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen is 56.08: 43:75: 0.17, Which indicates high purity of this sample. The XPS characterization gives consistent conclusion with respect to the EDS, FTIR and XRD study. Fig. S3 displays the XPS spectrum of the diamond QDs. The spectrum contains two very strong signals of C 1s and O 1s. And there is also a weak N signal. The atomic concentration ratio of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen is 57.10: 0.90: 42.00, which is very close to the result of the EDS. The trace nitrogen signal of EDS and XPS spectra may be due to the raw material impure. Fig. 3c shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s and it contains three peaks. The strong peak at 284.1 eV is ascribed to the sp3-hybridized carbon atoms in diamond [50,51], and this confirms the diamond structure of the nanoparticles. The peak at 286.0 eV is attributed to C–O–C. The 288.5 eV signal arises from CO [52]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 3d) consists of a strong peak at 532.2 eV and a weak shoulder peak at about 533.8 eV. The 532.2 eV signal arises from O(CO) and C–O–C [53]. The weak signal at 533.8 eV is attributed to O(CO) [43]. The results achieved here about the surface of the diamond QDs contributes greatly to understand their luminescence mechanisms.

Fig. 4 a shows the PL spectra of the diamond QDs aqueous solution. The typical spectrum comprises three distinct emission bands: a blue band at around 473 nm, a green band at 558 nm, and a red band at 658 nm. The diamond QDs exhibit the excitation-independent behavior, suggesting their origins of defect states rather than interband transitions. It is worth noting that the emission intensity corresponding to the excitation wavelength between 280 and 340 nm is quite weak. By magnifying the PL spectrum of that interval, in addition to the peak at 473 and 558 nm, there is a violet band centered at 432 nm (Fig. 4b). The PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the diamond QDs comprise three distinct peaks centered at around 374, 425, and 512 nm (Fig. 4c). Note that the intensity of the excitation wavelength centered between 280 nm and 340 nm is so weak that it tends to be a straight line. Comparing the variation laws of the emission and excitation bands, and considering the correspondence between the photon energy of PL peak and that of PLE peak, we conclude that the excitation bands at 320, 374, 425, and 512 nm correspond separately to the emission bands at 432, 473, 558, and 658 nm. Fig. 4d displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of diamond QDs. Relatively obvious, the spectra composed of two absorption intervals. The absorption bands range from 253 to 315 nm is ascribed to the π π* transition of the conjugated CC bonds. Both n π* (C(O)O and CC–CO) and π π* (CC) contribute to the absorption bands ranged 320 from 410 nm.

To further study the fluorescence characteristics of the diamond QDs, the variation curvet of the Stokes shift and PL intensity versus excitation wavelength was conducted. Fig. 5 a shows the Stokes shift versus excitation wavelength for the diamond QDs. Clearly, the sample comprises three distinct regions: 280–340 nm, 360–380 nm, and 400–500 nm. The curve possesses three variation trends, implying three different luminescence origins herein. That is, the emission band at 432 nm originates from one type of surface defect, the 473 nm emission band arises from a second-type surface defect, and the 558 and 658 nm stem from another type of surface defect. Fig. 5b shows peak intensity versus excitation wavelength for each emission band of the diamond QDs. The luminescence intensity at around 473 nm is relatively strong, and it makes a great superposition contribution to the luminescence of the adjacent 432 and 558 nm emission bands, so that the curves for the four emission bands all acquire a maximum at around 374 nm. Particularly the weaker emission band for 432 share the same variation trend with the 473 nm emission. Actually, the 432-nm emission has the other maxima at about 320 nm. Besides, the 558 and 658 nm emission bands share a similar variation rules in the whole band, indicating identical luminescence origin herein. Clearly, these two curves have three maxima at around 374, 425, and 512 nm, respectively, corresponding to the PLE peaks. Hence the information excitation from Fig. 5b strongly supports our deduction about the correspondence between excitation and emission.

Our early studies have shown that the emission at around 435 nm arises from the COOH-related surface defects, the emission at around 470 nm stems from the C–O–C-related surface defect, and the peaks at 527 and 614 nm originates from the C(O)O-related surface defects [50]. It is widely known that the carboxylic acids (COOH-) are impossible to exist under alkaline conditions, so the emission at 432 nm is severely inhibited. The (C(O)O-related functional group can be stable under alkaline conditions without heat-treatment, while the C–O–C-related functional group has always been a stable presence. As a result, the emission intensity at around 473, 588, and 658 nm is considerable high. Finally, the light absorption/emission at 374/473 nm arises from the C–O–C-related surface defects. The absorptions/emissions at around 425/558 and 512/658 nm stem from the C(O)O-related surface defects, and addition of conjugated CC to the C(O)O group on the nanoparticles surface causes remarkable red shift of the light absorption and emission.

In summary, we have synthesized the diamond QDs under room-temperature and studied their luminescence properties. The diamond QDs exhibit multicolor luminescence. The combined structural and optical characterizations confirm that the existence of various types of luminescent oxygen-related surface defects in the diamond QDs. The light absorption/emission at 374/473 nm arise from the C–O–C-related surface defects. The absorptions/emissions at around 425/558 and 512/658 nm stem from the C(O)O and CC–C(O)O-related surface defects, respectively. These findings provide a reliable way to understand the luminescence mechanism from the oxygen-related defects states of some nanoparticles.

Wenxia Zhang: Methodology, Design of experiment, Measurement and Analysis, Writing – original draft preparation; Zhen Wang: Writing-Reviewing and Editing; Yuchan Wang: Journal selection and Submission guidance; Suzhen Yuan: Auxiliary test analysis; Li Zhang: Assist article submission; Fei Qi: Overall regulatory and Writing guidance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (Nos. Cstc2018jcyjAX0547, cstc2018jcyjAX0462, cstc2018jcyjAX0560, and cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0124), the National Natural Science Foundation China (Nos.61804020 and 61801061), the Science and Technology Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. KJ QN201900643).
